original_abstract:
We introduce BEAST 2, an open-source, extensible software platform designed for Bayesian evolutionary analysis. This platform represents a comprehensive redesign of the widely used BEAST 1, specifically addressing structural limitations that emerged as the original software grew. A primary focus of this redesign was to overcome the lack of post-deployment extensibility. To achieve this, BEAST 2 features a robust package management system, enabling third-party developers to implement new functionalities that users can install directly without waiting for a core software release. 

We showcase this modular architecture through several recently published models, including birth-death-sampling tree priors, phylodynamic tools, and model-averaging techniques for substitution models and site partitioning. Another significant advancement is the platform’s ability to read and write the complete state of the MCMC chain to disk. This capability simplifies sharing between software instances and facilitates efficient checkpointing, providing better support for multi-processor and high-performance computing environments. Finally, we have achieved tighter integration between the analysis engine and the user interface (BEAUti 2). By utilizing a shared XML format and a template-based mechanism, new packages can now be seamlessly incorporated into the graphical interface, significantly enhancing user accessibility.
humanized_abstract:
BEAST 2 provides a new, open-source, highly flexible software framework for Bayesian evolutionary analysis. It is a major redesign of BEAST 1 and addresses the specific design problems that arose during the growth of BEAST 1. One of these problems was lack of extensibility after a release. In BEAST 2 we have implemented a comprehensive package management system allowing third-party developers to add new analysis methods, priors, models, trees searches, MCMC strategies and summary plugins, which can be downloaded by users and used in BEAST 2 without needing a new BEAST 2 release.

We demonstrate the modularity of BEAST2 through a few examples of published BEAST2 analyses using such as: • models including birth-death-sampling tree priors • phylodynamic analyses • model averaged substitution models and site partitions We have also implemented the full write and read of the MCMC state to disk (between programme instances) which can be used to share between instances of the programme or be used for checkpointing within a programme (for multi processor environments or for high performance computing). Also as part of BEAST2 we have done more work in getting the analysis engine and BEAUti 2 working closer together - such as sharing the same XML that is read from the file by the analysis engine, and using templates to make it easier to integrate a new module into BEAUti2 allowing users a simpler way of using new models.

Also as part of BEAST2 we have done more work in getting the analysis engine and BEAUti 2 working closer together - such as sharing the same XML that is read from the file by the analysis engine, and using templates to make it easier to integrate a new module into BEAUti2 allowing users a simpler way of using new models.
document.input:
We introduce BEAST 2, an open-source, extensible software platform designed for Bayesian evolutionary analysis. This platform represents a comprehensive redesign of the widely used BEAST 1, specifically addressing structural limitations that emerged as the original software grew. A primary focus of this redesign was to overcome the lack of post-deployment extensibility. To achieve this, BEAST 2 features a robust package management system, enabling third-party developers to implement new functionalities that users can install directly without waiting for a core software release. 

We showcase this modular architecture through several recently published models, including birth-death-sampling tree priors, phylodynamic tools, and model-averaging techniques for substitution models and site partitioning. Another significant advancement is the platform’s ability to read and write the complete state of the MCMC chain to disk. This capability simplifies sharing between software instances and facilitates efficient checkpointing, providing better support for multi-processor and high-performance computing environments. Finally, we have achieved tighter integration between the analysis engine and the user interface (BEAUti 2). By utilizing a shared XML format and a template-based mechanism, new packages can now be seamlessly incorporated into the graphical interface, significantly enhancing user accessibility.
